skill.name: data-exploration-tool
skill.description: Guides systematic database and table profiling in DBX Studio, including schema discovery, table statistics, relationship review, and data quality checks.
skill.summary: Explore schemas, profile tables, and summarize data quality in DBX Studio.
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks, SQL examples, weak-crypto strings, and a schema relationship example. Review found no executable code, no shell execution, no cryptographic implementation, no system reconnaissance, and no prompt injection text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Tool References
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the cited lines are markdown inline code, a numbered workflow, or SQL code fences. No executable shell command is defined in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown syntax and named DBX Studio tool calls. There is no Ruby code, shell invocation, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQL Examples Are Not External Commands
low_findings.description: The query examples show read-only profiling patterns for row counts, null rates, distinct values, and date ranges. They are documentation examples, not executed commands inside the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SQL is displayed in markdown fences and does not include mutation, filesystem access, networking, or shell execution. It still requires normal database permissions when used.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Match
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, but the cited lines contain ordinary words or SQL ordering text. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function, or encryption operation appears in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto API or algorithm name in context. The matches appear to come from substrings such as description, describe, or DESC.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Schema Relationship Example
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert points to an example database relationship between users and orders. It does not inspect the host system or collect operating system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a database schema example only. No commands, host identifiers, environment data, or reconnaissance workflow are present.

content.user_title: Profile Database Tables in DBX Studio
content.value_statement: Teams often need a fast, repeatable way to understand unfamiliar database tables. This skill guides schema discovery, table profiling, relationship review, and data quality summaries in DBX Studio.
actual_capabilities: Guides schema discovery with read_schema and describe_table workflows.
Profiles table size, column types, sample rows, null rates, and value distributions.
Suggests ways to infer relationships from foreign key naming patterns.
Provides SQL examples for row counts, fill rates, value frequency, and date ranges.
Defines a simple color scoring model for table completeness.
Specifies a structured exploration summary with tables, relationships, quality flags, and next queries.
limitations: Requires access to DBX Studio tools and the target database.
Relationship discovery is heuristic unless foreign keys are explicitly defined.
Sample data may not expose rare values or outliers.
The skill provides guidance and examples, not a standalone profiling engine.

use_cases.title: Map a New Database
use_cases.description: Create an initial table inventory with row counts, key columns, and likely relationships.

use_cases.title: Check Data Completeness
use_cases.description: Review null rates and population levels before building reports or models.

use_cases.title: Prepare Handoff Notes
use_cases.description: Summarize tables, relationships, quality flags, and useful follow-up queries for teammates.

prompt_templates.title: Explore One Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the data-exploration-tool to profile the public schema. Start with table discovery, then summarize the main tables and likely relationships.

prompt_templates.title: Profile a Table
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the data-exploration-tool to inspect the orders table. Include row count, important columns, sample data patterns, null rates, and suggested next questions.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Data Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the data-exploration-tool to assess data completeness for customer and transaction tables. Highlight columns with low fill rates and explain reporting risks.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Relationship Map
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the data-exploration-tool to infer relationships across the database. Compare identifier columns, describe join paths, and list queries that would validate each link.

output_examples.input: Profile the public schema and tell me which tables matter most.
output: Tables: customers, orders, order_items, and products appear central by naming and relationship patterns.
Key relationships: orders likely links to customers, and order_items likely links to orders and products.
Data quality flags: review customer contact fields and product category fill rates before reporting.
Suggested next queries: validate foreign key joins and check date ranges for recent activity.

output_examples.input: Check the users table for quality issues.
output: Completeness: core identifiers are expected to be fully populated.
Potential issues: optional profile fields may have lower fill rates and need segmentation before analysis.
Next step: compare created date ranges with activity tables to confirm active user coverage.

output_examples.input: Prepare a summary after exploring the warehouse schema.
output: Summary: list each table with row counts, key columns, and primary business purpose.
Relationships: document likely joins and mark each one as confirmed or needs validation.
Quality flags: identify high-null columns, sparse dimensions, and tables with limited date coverage.

best_practices: Start with schema discovery before profiling individual tables.
Use row counts and null rates to prioritize deeper investigation.
Validate inferred relationships before using them in reporting or models.
anti_patterns: Do not assume columns ending in id always have valid relationships.
Do not treat sample rows as complete evidence of data quality.
Do not run broad profiling on sensitive tables without approved access.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps you explore database schemas, profile tables, review relationships, and summarize data quality in DBX Studio.

faq.question: Does it run database queries by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides a workflow and query examples that depend on available DBX Studio tools and permissions.

faq.question: Can it find table relationships?
faq.answer: It can suggest likely relationships from naming patterns and schema details. Confirm important joins before production use.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive data?
faq.answer: Use normal access controls and data handling rules. The skill does not include exfiltration logic, but profiling may expose samples.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should the final output include?
faq.answer: The skill asks for tables, key relationships, data quality flags, and suggested next queries.
